rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical carboxylation of cinnamate esters with CO2 at a metallic ring cathode with sacrificial Mg anode, affording benzylmalonate (dicarboxylic) and monocarboxylic ester products.
electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of the cinnamate ester C=C to a radical anion, which attacks CO2; a second cathodic one-electron reduction generates a carbanion that is carboxylated again or protonated.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic radical from protonation of B (C)
carboxylated radical anion D (from B + CO2)
α-ester radical E (from C + CO2, after e-)
carbanion F (from second reduction of D)
monocarboxylic acid product G (from E pathway)

electron_flow: Cathode delivers one electron to the cinnamate ester to form a radical anion, which either adds to CO2 (major) or is protonated to a benzylic radical (minor); a second cathodic electron reduces these to carbanions that are trapped by CO2 (giving dicarboxylate) or by H+ (giving monocarboxylate), while the Mg anode is sacrificially oxidized.
rate_determining_step: First one-electron reduction of the cinnamate ester to the radical anion

probe_reagents.name: CO2 saturation in CV experiment
probe_reagents.observation: First reduction peak shifts positive and current increases (ip/ip0 ≈ 1.76–1.96), indicating reaction of the electrogenerated radical anion with CO2.

notes: Two competing branches diverge at the radical anion B: nucleophilic addition to CO2 (major, leading to F and then dicarboxylic acid I or monocarboxylic acid H) vs protonation to benzylic radical C (minor, giving monocarboxylic acid G after further reduction/CO2 capture). Authors argue carbanion F is sterically hindered, so H+ trapping outcompetes a second CO2 addition, accounting for the mixture of mono- and dicarboxylic products.
